← Home

Architecture

A deep dive into the SWE-Squad pipeline, feedback loops, memory system, and provider architecture.

Full Pipeline

SWE-Squad automates the entire incident lifecycle through a five-stage pipeline. Each stage contains focused sub-steps that handle a specific concern, from raw log ingestion all the way to validated deployment.

flowchart TD
    subgraph Monitor["🔍 Monitor"]
        M1[Log Ingestion]
        M2[Pattern Detection]
        M3[Alert Generation]
    end
    subgraph Triage["⚖️ Triage"]
        T1[Severity Classification]
        T2[Deduplication]
        T3[Assignment]
    end
    subgraph Investigate["🔬 Investigate"]
        I1[Codebase Exploration]
        I2[Root Cause Analysis]
        I3[Context Assembly]
    end
    subgraph Fix["🔧 Fix"]
        F1[Code Generation]
        F2[Test Generation]
        F3[Self-Review]
    end
    subgraph Gate["🛡️ Stability Gate"]
        G1[Unit Tests]
        G2[Lint & Type Check]
        G3[Security Scan]
        G4[Smoke Tests]
    end
    Monitor --> Triage --> Investigate --> Fix --> Gate
    Gate -- Pass --> Deploy[🚀 Deploy]
    Gate -- Fail --> Investigate
Monitor — Ingests logs and metrics from production systems, detects anomalous patterns, and generates structured alerts for downstream processing.
Triage — Classifies each alert by severity, deduplicates recurring issues to avoid duplicate work, and assigns the incident to the appropriate AI agent.
Investigate — Explores the relevant codebase, performs root-cause analysis, and assembles the context needed to produce an accurate fix.
Fix — Generates targeted code patches, produces corresponding tests, and runs a self-review step to catch obvious issues before validation.
Stability Gate — Validates the proposed fix through unit tests, linting, type checking, security scanning, and smoke tests. Only fixes that pass all checks proceed to deployment.

Fix Loop

When the Stability Gate rejects a fix, the system does not give up. Instead, it enters an iterative feedback loop: the agent reviews the specific failures, adjusts its approach, and generates an improved patch.

flowchart LR
    Fix --> Gate[Stability Gate]
    Gate -- Pass --> Deploy
    Gate -- Fail --> Review[Review Failures]
    Review --> Adjust[Adjust Approach]
    Adjust --> Fix

The loop is bounded by a retry limit. After a maximum of three attempts, the system escalates the incident to a human engineer along with the full diagnostic trace, ensuring no issue is silently abandoned. Each retry carries forward the failure context so the agent avoids repeating the same mistake.

Semantic Memory

SWE-Squad maintains a semantic memory store that accumulates knowledge across incidents. Every incoming issue is matched against this store to surface relevant historical context before the agent begins work.

flowchart TD
    Input[Incoming Issue] --> Memory[Semantic Memory Store]
    Memory --> |Relevant Context| Agent[AI Agent]
    Agent --> |New Findings| Memory
    Memory --> |Lessons Learned| Knowledge[Knowledge Base]
    Knowledge --> |Historical Patterns| Memory

As agents investigate and resolve issues, they write new findings back into the memory store. Over time, these findings are distilled into a persistent knowledge base of lessons learned and recurring patterns. This means the system becomes progressively faster and more accurate: previously seen error signatures are recognized immediately, and the agent can skip redundant exploration steps.

Provider Plugin Architecture

SWE-Squad isolates model-specific logic behind a provider interface. The BaseProvider abstract class defines the contract every provider must satisfy, while the ProviderRegistry manages registration and lookup at runtime.

flowchart TD
    subgraph Interface["Provider Interface"]
        PI[BaseProvider]
        PM[ProviderRegistry]
    end
    subgraph Implementations["Provider Implementations"]
        P1[OpenAIProvider]
        P2[AnthropicProvider]
        P3[LocalProvider]
        P4[CustomProvider]
    end
    PI --> P1
    PI --> P2
    PI --> P3
    PI --> P4
    PM --> PI

Each concrete provider — OpenAI, Anthropic, a local model server, or a custom implementation — extends BaseProvider and implements the same set of methods. Because the core pipeline depends only on the abstract interface, swapping or adding providers requires no changes to the surrounding logic. Teams can plug in new models, swap backends, or run entirely local inference without touching the pipeline code.

Agent Roles

SWE-Squad deploys specialized agents at each pipeline stage. Each agent has a defined role, specific inputs and outputs, and decision logic that governs its behavior.

flowchart TD
    subgraph Agents["Pipeline Agents"]
        Monitor["🔍 Monitor Agent"]
        Triage["⚖️ Triage Agent"]
        Investigator["🔬 Investigator Agent"]
        Developer["🔧 Developer Agent"]
        Ralph["🛡️ Ralph Wiggum<br/>(Stability Gate)"]
        Creative["💡 Creative Agent"]
        Distiller["🧠 Distiller Agent"]
    end
    Monitor -->|"Alert"| Triage
    Triage -->|"Classified Incident"| Investigator
    Investigator -->|"Root Cause + Context"| Developer
    Developer -->|"Patch + Tests"| Ralph
    Ralph -->|"Validated Fix"| Distiller
    Creative -->|"Alternative Approaches"| Developer
    Distiller -->|"Lessons Learned"| Memory["Semantic Memory"]
Monitor Agent — Continuously ingests logs, metrics, and error signals from production systems. Inputs: raw log streams, metric thresholds, alert rules. Outputs: structured alerts with severity hints. Decision logic: anomaly detection via pattern matching against known signatures and statistical baselines.
Triage Agent — Classifies each alert by severity and deduplicates against active incidents. Inputs: structured alerts from Monitor. Outputs: classified, deduplicated incident tickets. Decision logic: severity scored by error category, blast radius, and user impact; duplicates detected via semantic similarity against open tickets.
Investigator Agent — Performs deep root-cause analysis by exploring the codebase and tracing error paths. Inputs: classified incident ticket, codebase access, commit history, semantic memory. Outputs: root cause summary, relevant file list, context window for fix generation. Decision logic: follows call-stack traces, identifies recent commits touching failing paths, and cross-references with historical fixes.
Developer Agent — Generates targeted code patches and corresponding test cases. Inputs: root cause context from Investigator, file contents, project conventions. Outputs: code diff, test suite, self-review notes. Decision logic: applies minimal-diff strategy — prefers surgical edits over rewrites, and validates generated code against project lint and type rules before submission.
Ralph Wiggum (Stability Gate) — Validates every proposed fix through a comprehensive quality gate. Inputs: code diff and tests from Developer. Outputs: pass/fail verdict with detailed failure report. Decision logic: runs unit tests, lint checks, type verification, security scanning (OWASP top 10), and smoke tests. Rejects any fix that fails a single check and returns failure context to the Developer Agent for the next retry.
Creative Agent — Activated when the Developer Agent exhausts its retry budget without a passing fix. Inputs: accumulated failure context from previous attempts. Outputs: alternative solution strategies and unconventional approaches. Decision logic: breaks out of the local optimization loop by proposing fundamentally different solution paths — a different algorithm, a different module boundary, or a configuration-based fix instead of a code change.
Distiller Agent — Runs post-resolution to extract reusable knowledge from each completed incident. Inputs: full incident trace, root cause, fix diff, and test results. Outputs: condensed lesson entries written to semantic memory. Decision logic: identifies which parts of the investigation were novel versus redundant, and stores only the new knowledge that would accelerate future resolutions.

Ticket Lifecycle

Every incident follows a well-defined state machine from first alert to resolution. The ticket lifecycle ensures no issue falls through the cracks — every state transition is tracked, timed, and auditable.

stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> New: Alert Ingested
    New --> Triaged: Classified
    Triaged --> Investigating: Agent Assigned
    Investigating --> InDevelopment: Root Cause Found
    InDevelopment --> InReview: Fix Submitted
    InReview --> Deployed: All Checks Pass
    InReview --> InDevelopment: Checks Fail
    InDevelopment --> Escalated: Retry Limit Hit
    Escalated --> InDevelopment: Human Provides Guidance
    Deployed --> [*]: Resolved

Tickets move linearly through the pipeline under normal conditions. When the Stability Gate rejects a fix, the ticket loops back to In Development. If the retry budget is exhausted, the ticket escalates to a human engineer while remaining in the active queue. Once deployed, the ticket transitions to Resolved and feeds its findings back into semantic memory.

Model Routing & Escalation

SWE-Squad routes each task to the most cost-effective model capable of handling it. Simple classification runs on Haiku, investigation and patch generation use Sonnet, and complex architectural decisions invoke Opus.

flowchart TD
    Input[Incoming Incident] --> Classify{Complexity?}
    Classify -->|Simple| Haiku[Haiku<br/>~$0.25/1M tokens]
    Classify -->|Standard| Sonnet[Sonnet<br/>~$3.00/1M tokens]
    Classify -->|Complex| Opus[Opus<br/>~$15.00/1M tokens]
    Haiku --> Result{Pass?}
    Sonnet --> Result
    Opus --> Result
    Result -->|Yes| Deploy[Deploy Fix]
    Result -->|No + Retries Left| Upgrade{Upgrade Model?}
    Upgrade -->|Yes| NextModel[Next Tier Up]
    Upgrade -->|No| SameModel[Same Model + New Context]
    NextModel --> Result
    SameModel --> Result
    Result -->|No + Retries Exhausted| Escalate[Escalate to Human]

The auto-escalation path is equally important: if a model fails to produce a passing fix within its retry budget, the system first tries upgrading to the next model tier. If all tiers are exhausted, the incident escalates to a human engineer with the full diagnostic trace. This ensures every issue eventually reaches the right level of expertise — whether AI or human.

Multi-Team Scoping

SWE-Squad supports multiple engineering teams within a single deployment. Each team is scoped to its own codebase boundaries, agent configurations, and escalation policies — while sharing a common memory store for cross-team learning.

flowchart TD
    subgraph Platform["SWE-Squad Platform"]
        Router[Team Router]
        Memory[Shared Memory Store]
        Router --> TeamA["Team Alpha<br/>Backend Services"]
        Router --> TeamB["Team Bravo<br/>Frontend & Mobile"]
        Router --> TeamC["Team Charlie<br/>Data Pipeline"]
        TeamA --> ScopeA["Scoped Codebase Access<br/>Service A, B, C"]
        TeamB --> ScopeB["Scoped Codebase Access<br/>Web, iOS, Android"]
        TeamC --> ScopeC["Scoped Codebase Access<br/>ETL, ML Pipeline"]
        ScopeA --> Memory
        ScopeB --> Memory
        ScopeC --> Memory
    end

The Team Router directs incoming alerts to the correct team based on service ownership metadata. Within each team, agents operate within strictly scoped codebase boundaries — a backend agent cannot access frontend repositories, and vice versa. However, the shared semantic memory store allows lessons learned by one team to benefit others. Cross-team incidents (e.g., an API change that breaks a frontend consumer) are detected by the Router and broadcast to all affected teams simultaneously.

What Happens to a Log Line

Follow a single error log line on its journey from ingestion to deployment.

1
Ingestion

A production service emits an error log line — perhaps a NullPointerException or a timeout stack trace. The Monitor stage ingests this line in real time alongside thousands of other log entries, normalizing its format and attaching metadata such as service name, timestamp, and environment.

2

Pattern Detection scans the incoming stream against known anomaly signatures. The error line matches a threshold breach or a novel pattern, and the system flags it as anomalous, promoting it from a raw log entry to a structured alert.

3

Triage takes over the alert. It classifies severity (critical, high, medium, low), checks whether an identical or similar issue is already being tracked, and deduplicates if necessary to avoid parallel agents working the same problem.

4

The assignment step routes the incident to the right AI agent based on the service, language, and error category. Model routing ensures a fast Haiku-class model handles straightforward classification while a heavier Sonnet or Opus model is reserved for complex root-cause analysis.

5
Deep Investigation

The assigned agent enters the Investigate stage, exploring the codebase to locate the relevant files, tracing the error path through the call stack, and assembling a rich context window that includes surrounding code, recent commits, and any related historical fixes from the semantic memory store.

6

In the Fix stage, the agent generates a targeted code patch, writes corresponding unit tests, and runs a self-review pass to catch syntax errors, off-by-one mistakes, or obvious logic flaws before submitting.

7
Stability Gate

The proposed fix is handed to the Stability Gate, which runs the full validation suite: unit tests, linting, type checking, security scanning, and smoke tests. If any check fails, the fix is rejected and the loop restarts. If all checks pass, the gate opens and the fix is cleared for deployment.

8
Deployed

The validated fix is merged and deployed to production. The semantic memory store is updated with the new findings so that future encounters with the same pattern are resolved instantly — often without invoking a model at all.
